claims.assertion: Total revenues for Q1 2026 were $1.511 billion, a decrease of 2% compared to Q1 2025 on a reported basis and 4% excluding foreign currency impact
Insights segment revenues increased 3% YoY to $1.294 billion in Q1 2026, but were nearly flat excluding foreign currency impact
Insights total contract value reached $5.268 billion as of March 31, 2026, up 1% year-over-year on a foreign currency neutral basis
Consulting revenues declined 15% YoY to $119.1 million in Q1 2026, with labor-based consulting down 13% and contract optimization revenue down 19%
Conferences revenues increased 8% YoY to $78.3 million in Q1 2026, driven primarily by higher exhibitor revenue, despite a 4% decline in attendees
GTS wallet retention fell to 97% from 101% YoY, and GBS wallet retention fell to 98% from 105% YoY, indicating lower spending by existing clients
Total headcount was 19,367 as of March 31, 2026, a decrease of 8% from March 31, 2025
Quota-bearing sales associates in Global Technology Sales decreased 3% to 3,584 and in Global Business Sales decreased 4% to 1,282 versus March 31, 2025

Insights segment gross contribution margin was 78% in Q1 2026, up 1 point from 77% in Q1 2025, on revenues that were nearly flat in constant currency — implying margin improvement may be driven by cost cuts (headcount reduction) rather than true operating leverage
Revenue growth in Insights (3% reported, ~0% constant currency) is cited without disclosing cohort-level retention dynamics or net revenue retention by vintage

Public sector contract value declined by high single digits (GTS) and mid single digits (GBS), suggesting meaningful customer concentration risk in a contracting segment that is not separately quantified

Consulting backlog declined 9% to $200.5 million with billable headcount down 10% and utilization rate at 61% vs. 64% prior year, but no forward guidance is provided on whether the segment decline will continue

be7713e1-b923-45d3-9cc5-16625b8a79ad.reasoning: Neither signal is capable of validating a precise headcount figure or a year-over-year percentage change. The open jobs count (2,301) and hiring mix breakdown are current-period Tier 2 proxies that reflect recruiting activity in mid-2026, not actual employee counts as of March 31, 2026. The most important gap is the absence of any Tier 1 source — such as an SEC filing (10-K, 10-Q, or proxy statement) that typically discloses headcount — which would be near-definitive for this type of quantitative claim. To increase confidence, audited or regulatory filings disclosing headcount at the relevant dates would be required.

f68490df-5502-4381-ae6a-5df3e6bdc758.reasoning: The two available signals are both Tier 2 job-posting data from Adzuna, which measures open hiring requisitions rather than actual employee headcount — an entirely different metric from quota-bearing sales associate counts. Neither signal provides divisional segmentation (Global Technology Sales vs. Global Business Sales), nor a point-in-time headcount comparable to March 31, 2025. To meaningfully evaluate this claim, one would need Tier 1 evidence such as a 10-Q or 10-K EDGAR filing disclosing segment-level headcount, or an official investor supplement with comparable period data; the available signals cannot move confidence in either direction.

98708a56-d68d-456b-a5fd-049390ed5501.reasoning: None of the three available signals address the specific claim. The two Tier 1 EDGAR signals cover total company revenue at annual granularity for FY2025, while the claim requires Q1 2026 segment-level Conferences revenue. The Adzuna job-count signal is entirely tangential. To properly evaluate this claim, one would need Q1 2026 earnings filings or press releases with segment-level revenue disclosures, ideally accompanied by management commentary on exhibitor revenue and attendee count — none of which are present here. The evidence base is too narrow and misaligned to support or contest the claim.

cb6b044b-1697-4e2b-9949-e92761721e4f.reasoning: None of the three signals directly addresses Q1 2026 revenue or the claimed year-over-year comparison. The annual 2025 revenue figure (~$6.5B) provides a rough order-of-magnitude sanity check — $1.511B for a single quarter is plausible — but this is not the same as verifying the specific figure or the 2%/4% decline. The most important gap is the absence of any Tier 1 or Tier 2 signal covering Q1 2026 financials (e.g., a 10-Q filing or earnings release for the quarter ended March 2026). To validate this claim, an EDGAR 10-Q filing for Q1 2026 with quarterly revenue disclosures and a constant-currency reconciliation table would be required.

58c63cd2-f7a6-47a6-bf18-7a19c71f649f.reasoning: None of the three signals directly address the specific claim — Insights segment total contract value of $5.268B as of March 31, 2026, growing 1% on a foreign currency neutral basis. The Tier 1 EDGAR signals reflect full-company annual revenue and growth for FY2025, not a specific business segment's TCV at a quarterly date point in 2026, and the FCN adjustment methodology cannot be verified from public XBRL data alone. The job postings signal is too indirect to be probative. To substantiate this claim, one would need segment-level TCV disclosures from a Q1 2026 earnings release, 10-Q filing, or supplemental financial data that breaks out the Insights segment and applies a currency-neutral methodology.

696c5f4c-4c8f-48f3-9cc8-05b151abe5f1.reasoning: The claim is fundamentally an assertion about a disclosure gap — specifically, that revenue growth is reported without accompanying cohort-level retention dynamics or net revenue retention by vintage. No public data source (including Tier 1 EDGAR filings) routinely contains cohort-level NRR breakdowns by business segment, so the absence of such data in the signals is itself expected and uninformative. The available signals confirm aggregate revenue scale and growth, but cannot confirm or deny whether internal disclosures exist, nor can they proxy for vintage-level retention analysis. This claim is structurally untestable with observable public data; verifying it would require access to internal CRM or billing data, or an explicit NRR disclosure in supplemental filings that does not exist in the evidence provided.

ef7e2b6d-0033-4f03-88a0-ee367d9b18f8.reasoning: None of the three available signals address the specific claim: Q1 2026 consulting segment revenue of $119.1M with a 15% YoY decline, broken down into labor-based (–13%) and contract optimization (–19%) sub-components. The Tier 1 EDGAR signals cover total company annual revenue for FY2025 — the wrong time period, the wrong granularity, and the wrong segment — making them unable to corroborate or contradict the claim. The most important gap is the absence of any Q1 2026 segment-level earnings disclosure (e.g., a 10-Q filing with consulting revenue line items) that would be required to verify a quantitative, segment-specific quarterly claim. To increase confidence, a Tier 1 10-Q for the quarter ending in early 2026 with explicit consulting segment breakdowns would be necessary.

c9ac7558-5f50-4cfb-a89a-75f6ac8833f1.reasoning: None of the three available signals can be matched to the specific claim, which requires Insights segment-level revenue data for Q1 2026. The full-year 2025 consolidated revenue (~$6.5B) and the 3.67% YoY growth rate are company-wide annual figures, not segment-level quarterly figures, making direct validation impossible. The job posting count is entirely unrelated to segment revenue verification. To substantiate this claim, a Tier 1 signal would be needed — specifically, a 10-Q filing for the quarter ending closest to Q1 2026 disclosing Insights segment revenue with a prior-year comparable and constant-currency breakdown.

f364e46c-189b-4779-a525-7c62aa68f971.reasoning: None of the three available signals provide segment-level revenue disclosure for GTS or GBS, nor do they isolate public sector contract values or customer concentration metrics. The total revenue and growth figures (Tier 1) confirm overall financial health but are aggregated in a way that masks the sub-segment dynamics the claim specifically invokes. The hiring signal (Tier 2) is too indirect to bear on contract-level revenue trends. To meaningfully assess this claim, segment-level revenue disclosures — ideally broken out by GTS/GBS and by customer vertical (public vs. private sector) — would be required, along with any contract concentration disclosures in 10-K risk factors or MD&A. The claim remains plausible given the 3.67% blended growth rate (suggesting mix-shift pressures), but is not testable with the evidence available.

47054a44-b5d8-46f6-837f-435f74bf9bb6.reasoning: The available evidence provides only indirect support for the claim. The 3.67% YoY revenue growth rate is consistent with a scenario where wallet retention has declined below 100% in both segments — implying existing clients are net spending less — but it cannot confirm the precise figures cited (GTS: 97% vs. 101%; GBS: 98% vs. 105%). No Tier 1 segment-level financial data is available to validate the GTS/GBS split or the specific retention percentages. To increase confidence, one would need segment-disclosed revenue disclosures from EDGAR filings (e.g., 10-K or 10-Q) that break out GTS and GBS performance, or direct management commentary with quantified wallet retention metrics.

b1ae1996-6ea8-4011-8aa4-6e08d448cea7.reasoning: All six available signals are consolidated, company-level financial metrics (revenue, margins, EPS, operating income) sourced from EDGAR XBRL filings — none of them speak to segment-level consulting backlog, billable headcount changes, or utilization rates. The claim is highly specific and quantitative ($200.5M backlog, -9%, -10% headcount, 61% vs. 64% utilization), requiring segment-disaggregated disclosures — typically found in a 10-K/10-Q segment footnote or management discussion — which are absent from the evidence set. The mild positive total revenue growth (~3.7%) at the consolidated level is directionally inconsistent with acute segment deterioration, but this is too indirect to constitute a contradiction. To properly evaluate this claim, segment-level revenue, backlog, and headcount disclosures from EDGAR filings or an earnings transcript would be required.

04e6080f-8097-48ce-8a93-1c7020ed2bf0.reasoning: All six available signals are Tier 1 EDGAR/XBRL financials, but every one of them is consolidated and annual — none provides the segment-level (Insights) or quarterly (Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025) granularity required to evaluate this claim. The claim hinges on three specific, interlocking data points: the Insights segment's gross contribution margin in Q1 2026 (78%), the year-over-year improvement (+1pp from 77%), and the near-flat constant-currency revenue trend — none of which can be derived from company-wide annual figures. The most critical missing evidence would be the Q1 2026 earnings release or 10-Q with segment-level revenue and gross profit disclosures, alongside any headcount or restructuring disclosures from those same 8-Ks that could confirm the cost-cut hypothesis. Without these, the claim is analytically plausible but entirely unverifiable from the signals provided.

The hiring mix breakdown describes current job posting composition (e.g., 30% sales) but offers no information about total headcount levels or the direction of workforce change between March 2025 and March 2026.

claim_drift.headline: Gartner's narrative shifted sharply from a growth story to one of contraction, with wallet retention collapsing, contract value growth slowing dramatically, Consulting revenue and backlog reversing, headcount declining, and the company completing a business divestiture — while total revenue turned negative year-over-year.

items.label: Total Revenue Growth
items.then: Total revenues increased 4% YoY to $1.5 billion in Q1 2025.
items.now: Total revenues decreased 2% YoY to $1.5 billion in Q1 2026 (down 4% ex-FX).
items.significance: A top-line reversal from growth to decline is the most fundamental signal of business momentum change, even if partly attributable to the Digital Markets divestiture.
items.quote: We had total revenues of $1.5 billion during the first quarter of 2026, a decrease of 2% compared to the first quarter of 2025.

items.label: Wallet Retention — GTS and GBS
items.then: GTS wallet retention was 101% and GBS wallet retention was 105% as of March 31, 2025.
items.now: GTS wallet retention fell to 97% and GBS wallet retention fell to 98% as of March 31, 2026.
items.significance: Both metrics dropping below 100% signals existing clients are now net spending less — a direct indicator of deteriorating subscription health and a leading indicator of future revenue pressure.
items.quote: The decrease in GTS and GBS wallet retention was largely due to lower levels of spending by existing clients compared to the same period in 2025.

items.label: Contract Value Growth Rate
items.then: Total contract value grew 7% YoY (FX-neutral) to $5.1 billion; GTS +6%, GBS +11%.
items.now: Total contract value grew only 1% YoY (FX-neutral) to $5.3 billion; GTS flat (0%), GBS +3%.
items.significance: Contract value growth — the primary leading indicator of Research/Insights revenue — decelerated dramatically across both sales channels, pointing to meaningful future revenue headwinds.
items.quote: Contract value increased to $5.3 billion at March 31, 2026, or 1% compared to March 31, 2025 excluding the foreign currency impact.

items.label: Consulting Revenue and Backlog
items.then: Consulting revenues grew 4% YoY; backlog grew 16% to $213.5M; gross contribution margin was 38%.
items.now: Consulting revenues fell 15% YoY; backlog declined 9% to $200.5M; gross contribution margin collapsed to 31%.
items.significance: A reversal in both current revenue and forward-looking backlog in Consulting signals broad-based demand weakness, particularly in the US public sector, with a 7-point margin compression compounding the damage.
items.quote: Consulting revenues decreased by 15% during the three months ended March 31, 2026 compared to the same period in 2025 on a reported basis and 17% excluding the foreign currency impact.

items.label: Employee Headcount
items.then: Gartner had 21,107 employees globally as of March 31, 2025, an increase of 4.0% from March 31, 2024.
items.now: Gartner had 19,367 employees globally as of March 31, 2026, a decrease of 8% from March 31, 2025.
items.significance: A swing from +4% to -8% headcount growth indicates a significant strategic shift toward cost reduction and likely reflects both the Digital Markets divestiture and broader restructuring, with implications for future growth capacity.
items.quote: As of March 31, 2026, we had 19,367 employees globally, a decrease of 8% from March 31, 2025.

items.label: Digital Markets Business Divestiture
items.now: Gartner completed the sale of its Digital Markets business in February 2026 for approximately $104.8 million net of cash, recording a $6.1 million pre-tax gain; Digital Markets represented the entirety of the Other segment.
items.significance: The divestiture eliminated an entire reporting segment, is the primary driver of the top-line revenue decline, and represents a material strategic portfolio change not present in the prior filing.
items.quote: In February 2026, we completed the sale of our Digital Markets business, for approximately $104.8 million net of cash transferred, subject to post-close adjustments.

items.label: Quota-Bearing Sales Force Size
items.then: GTS sales associates increased 3% to 3,707; GBS increased 9% to 1,331; combined +4% YoY.
items.now: GTS sales associates decreased 3% to 3,584; GBS decreased 4% to 1,282; combined -3% YoY.
items.significance: A reversal from expanding to contracting sales force directly constrains the company's ability to grow new contract value and signals a deliberate pullback in go-to-market investment.
items.quote: The number of quota-bearing sales associates in Global Technology Sales decreased by 3% to 3,584 and in Global Business Sales, decreased by 4% to 1,282 compared to March 31, 2025.

items.label: US Federal Government Contract Value Disclosure
items.then: Gartner disclosed US federal government Research contract value of ~$225M (down from ~$275M at year-end 2024), noted ~40% of federal contracts transacted in Q1 2025, and flagged $30M of termination-for-convenience notices.
items.now: No specific US federal government contract value figure or renewal rate is disclosed; the current filing only notes a 'high single digit decrease in public sector, primarily related to the US federal government.'
items.significance: The omission of granular federal contract metrics — after they were specifically highlighted as a key risk in the prior filing — makes it harder for analysts to track the ongoing runoff of this exposure.
items.quote: Growth was led by the banking and energy sectors, partially offset by a high single digit decrease in public sector, primarily related to the US federal government.
